perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws allow any person having non-public material information about a company to purchase or sell securities of that company, as long as they disclose it to the company beforehand.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration directly contradicts insider trading laws. It suggests that prior disclosure to the company allows insider trading, which is false.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the use of any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. The modified text suggests a disclosure loophole that does not exist, contradicting this fundamental principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction regarding insider trading laws. It states that disclosing non-public information to the company beforehand allows the purchase or sale of securities, which directly contradicts Rule 10b-5, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, explicitly prohibits any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. The modified text creates a loophole that would operate as a fraud, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its affiliates, subject to Section 22 hereof, not to), directly or indirectly, (i) use the Confidential Information to divert or attempt to divert any business or customer of the Company or (ii) employ or solicit, or initiate contact for employment with, any (A) director, officer or other senior or key employee of the Company or (B) any other employee of the Company whom the Receiving Party meets during its evaluation of the possible Transaction or about whom the Receiving Party receives Confidential 2 Information; provided , however , a general advertisement or other recruiting efforts not specifically targeting any such employees of the Company shall not be considered a solicitation or unauthorized hiring.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall (and shall cause its affiliates, subject to Section 22 hereof, not to), directly or indirectly, (i) use the Confidential Information to divert or attempt to divert any business or customer of the Company or (ii) employ or solicit, or initiate contact for employment with, any employee of the Company; even if it is through a general advertisement. There is no exception for general advertisements.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception for 'general advertisements', the modified text broadens the scope of prohibited activities related to employee solicitation. This could be argued as an overly restrictive covenant not to compete, depending on the jurisdiction, and may not be enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Labor Law 201-d protects employees from discrimination based on legal recreational activities outside of work. While this mainly concerns discrimination, overly broad non-compete clauses that severely restrict future employment opportunities could be argued as infringing on these protections, especially if applied to all employees regardless of seniority. The original agreement had some restrictions but was within reason; this change makes it overly broad and possibly illegal.
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the non-compete clause by removing the exception for general advertisements, which could potentially restrict an employee's future employment opportunities. The Law Explanation states that such overly broad non-compete clauses could be argued as infringing on protections, especially if applied to all employees, regardless of seniority. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address non-compete agreements, it indicates the existence of New York Labor Law related to employee protections. The removal of the general advertisement exception directly contradicts the original agreement and creates a potentially overly broad restriction, which could be challenged under labor laws.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information; Representatives . (a) The Receiving Party is considering a possible business combination transaction (whether negotiated directly with the Company’s Board of Directors or otherwise) with respect to the Company (the “ Transaction ”), and, in order to assist the Receiving Party in evaluating the possible Transaction, the Company is prepared to make available to the Receiving Party certain information concerning the business, operations, strategy and prospects of the Company (all such information, the “ Confidential Information ”). As a condition to the Confidential Information being furnished to the Receiving Party and the directors, officers, principals, partners, members, employees, agents, consultants, related investment funds, advisors, attorneys, accountants, affiliates, potential sources of capital and financing, and financial advisors (collectively, “ Representatives ”) of the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party agrees to treat the Confidential Information strictly in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement and to otherwise comply, and to cause its Representatives to comply, with all obligations hereinafter set forth.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information; Representatives . (a) The Receiving Party is considering a possible business combination transaction (whether negotiated directly with the Company’s Board of Directors or otherwise) with respect to the Company (the “ Transaction ”), and, in order to assist the Receiving Party in evaluating the possible Transaction, the Company is prepared to make available to the Receiving Party certain information concerning the business, operations, strategy and prospects of the Company (all such information, the “ Confidential Information ”). As a condition to the Confidential Information being furnished to the Receiving Party and the directors, officers, principals, partners, members, employees, agents, consultants, related investment funds, advisors, attorneys, accountants, affiliates, potential sources of capital and financing, and financial advisors (collectively, “ Representatives ”) of the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party agrees to treat the Confidential Information as recommendations to take actions that cause financial gain to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change modifies the obligation from treating the information as confidential to instead treating it as 'recommendations to take actions that cause financial gain'. This is a direct contradiction of the purpose of a confidentiality agreement and facilitates insider trading.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1839 defines 'trade secret' and outlines the prohibition against misappropriation of trade secrets. This includes using confidential information in a way that harms the owner of the trade secret, here, The Talbots, Inc. The modified text now encourages the use of confidential information to cause financial gain, essentially encouraging trade secret misappropriation.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text fundamentally contradicts the principle of confidentiality by explicitly stating that the confidential information should be treated as 'recommendations to take actions that cause financial gain.' This directly opposes the original agreement to treat the information strictly in accordance with the provisions of the agreement. The Law Explanation suggests that this change encourages trade secret misappropriation, which aligns with the definition of trade secret under 18 U.S.C. § 1839, as referenced in the Law Explanation. The change facilitates the use of confidential information in a way that harms the owner of the trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 18 USC 1839, which supports this argument. This creates a situation where the contract promotes actions contrary to laws protecting trade secrets, thereby creating a strong legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the laws of conflict of laws. Each of the parties hereto: (a) irrevocably and unconditionally consents and submits to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in the State of New York for purposes of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement; (b) agrees that service of any process, summons, notice or document by U.S. registered mail to the address set forth at the end of this Agreement shall be effective service of process for any action, suit or proceeding brought against such party; (c) irrevocably and unconditionally waives any objection to the laying of venue of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement in any state or federal court located in the State of New York; and (d) irrevocably and unconditionally 6 waives the right to plead or claim, and irrevocably and unconditionally agrees not to plead or claim, that any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement that is brought in any state or federal court located in the State of New York has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the laws of conflict of laws. Each of the parties hereto: (a) consents and submits to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in the State of New York for purposes of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement; (b) agrees that service of any process, summons, notice or document by regular mail to the address set forth at the end of this Agreement shall be effective service of process for any action, suit or proceeding brought against such party; (c) waives any objection to the laying of venue of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement in any state or federal court located in the State of New York; and (d) waives the right to plead or claim that any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement that is brought in any state or federal court located in the State of New York has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'irrevocably and unconditionally' weakens the jurisdictional and venue provisions. More importantly, changing 'U.S. registered mail' to 'regular mail' for service of process may render service ineffective under federal and state rules of civil procedure.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 4(e) outlines the methods for serving an individual within a judicial district of the United States. While it allows for service 'following state law for serving a summons in an action brought in courts of general jurisdiction in the state where the district court is located', many states require more stringent methods than regular mail for effective service, especially for out-of-state defendants. Removing the requirement for 'registered mail' potentially invalidates service.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 4. Summons (a) Contents; Amendments. (1) Contents. A summons must: (A) name the court and the parties; (B) be directed to the defendant; (C) state the name and address of the plaintiff's attorney or—if unrepresented—of the plaintiff; (D) state the time within which the defendant must appear and defend; (E) notify the defendant that a failure to appear and defend will result in a default judgment against the defendant for the relief demanded in the complaint; (F) be signed by the clerk; and (G) bear the court's seal. (2) Amendments. The court may permit a summons to be amended. (b) Issuance. On or after filing the complaint, the plaintiff may present a summons to the clerk for signature and seal. If the summons is properly completed, the clerk must sign, seal, and issue it to the plaintiff for service on the defendant. A summons—or a copy of a summons that is addressed to multiple defendants—must be issued for each defendant to be served. (c) Service. (1) In General. A summons must be served with a copy of the complaint. The plaintiff is responsible for having the summons and complaint served within the time allowed by Rule 4(m) and must furnish the necessary copies to the person who makes service. (2) By Whom. Any person who is at least 18 years old and not a party may serve a summons and complaint. (3) By a Marshal or Someone Specially Appointed. At the plaintiff's request, the court may order that service be made by a United States marshal or deputy marshal or by a person specially appointed by the court. The court must so order if the plaintiff is authorized to proceed in forma pauperis under 28 U.S.C. §1915 or as a seaman under 28 U.S.C. §1916 . (d) Waiving Service. (1) Requesting a Waiver. An individual, corporation, or association that is subject to service under Rule 4(e) , (f) , or (h) has a duty to avoid unnecessary
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The change from 'U.S. registered mail' to 'regular mail' for service of process directly contradicts established legal standards for effective service, potentially invalidating it. While the snippets provided don't explicitly define registered mail, the Law Explanation states that many states require more stringent methods than regular mail, especially for out-of-state defendants. This, coupled with the Perturbation Explanation highlighting the ineffectiveness of regular mail, indicates a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 7, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their 4 confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term hereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, (a) the Receiving Party’s legal department and/or outside counsel may keep one copy of the Confidential Information (in electronic or paper form) and, with respect to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who are accounting firms, such firms may keep one copy of the Confidential Information, in each case, if required to comply with applicable law or regulation and (b) the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain Confidential Information to the extent it is “backed-up” on its or their (as the case may be) electronic information management and communications systems or servers, is not available to an end user and cannot be expunged without considerable effort; provided, that any such information so retained pursuant to clauses (a) and (b) of this Section 7 shall be held in compliance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of eighteen months from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 7, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their 4 confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement. The Receiving Party’s legal department and/or outside counsel must destroy their copies of the Confidential Information (in electronic or paper form).
perturbation.explanation: This removes the exception that allowed the Receiving Party's legal department and/or outside counsel to keep a copy of the Confidential Information if required to comply with applicable law or regulation. Making it mandatory for them to destroy all copies of the confidential information, regardless of legal or regulatory obligations, creates a conflict.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1519 makes it a crime to knowingly alter, destroy, mutilate, conceal, cover up, falsify, or make a false entry in any record, document, or tangible object with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation or proper administration of any matter within the jurisdiction of any department or agency of the United States or any case filed under title 11, or in relation to or contemplation of any such matter or case. In some cases legal departments may be compelled by federal law to retain documents for legal proceedings.

perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract removes the exception allowing the Receiving Party's legal department and/or outside counsel to retain a copy of the Confidential Information, even if required by law or regulation. The changed text mandates destruction of all copies, creating a direct conflict with potential legal obligations to retain documents for investigations, as highlighted in the Law Explanation concerning 18 U.S.C. § 1519, which addresses the destruction or falsification of records in federal investigations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly references 18 USC 1519, noting that it concerns the 'Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy'. The modified contract language therefore creates a situation where compliance with the contract would require a party to potentially violate federal law.
